quiz.prompt: Your pole's £18k readiness assessment barely breaks even, and a partner suggests either raising its price to a 'proper' margin or dropping it from the catalogue. What is the right way to evaluate this service?

choices.text: Judge it by what it converts into — it is the cheap, low-risk entry rung of a funnel, priced to maximise an easy "yes" into the build and the run, and its real return is the LTV of the relationship it opens.
choices.explanation: Exactly. The offer is a funnel, not a price list. A £18k door-opener that reliably converts into £200k+ of project work plus a recurring retainer has a return that has nothing to do with its own line in the P&L. Price it for conversion, not for margin.

choices.text: Raise the price until it hits the same margin target as every other service — a pole should not run loss-making lines.
choices.explanation: A uniform margin target misreads the ladder. Rung-1 services are deliberately priced at or near cost — sometimes below — because they exist to *earn the build*, qualify the client, and produce the backlog that scopes rungs 2-4. Pricing them 'properly' kills the easy yes.

choices.text: Drop it and lead with the production build, where the real revenue is.
choices.explanation: You rarely win the £400k platform on day one. The cheap assessment de-risks the big bet *for both sides*: the client buys a small bet before a big one, and you qualify them before committing delivery capacity. Removing the entry rung removes the way in.

choices.text: Keep it but strip the deliverable down until the delivery cost makes it profitable on its own.
choices.explanation: The module is explicit: make the assessment genuinely useful *even if they never buy more* — that's what builds trust and referrals, and every assessment doubles as a potential case study. A thin deliverable saves cost and destroys the conversion the service exists for.

quiz.takeaway: Value an entry sale by the relationship it opens (LTV ≈ annual contract value × expected years), not by its own fee — the assessment is an option on a multi-year relationship.

quiz.prompt: Why does the module call rung 4 (run / managed service) "where a consultancy becomes a business"?

choices.text: Recurring retainer revenue is predictable and compounds, unlike project revenue which is lumpy and must be re-sold every quarter — and the run seat is the trusted position that surfaces the next assessment.
choices.explanation: Right on both halves. A book of managed-service retainers is what a P&L (or an acquirer) values most, and the operating relationship is the engine of land-and-expand: the team running the system sees the next opportunity (fraud, pricing) before anyone else.

choices.text: Run contracts command the highest day rates in the catalogue, so they maximise revenue per consultant.
choices.explanation: It isn't about rates — retainers are usually modest per month (£9k/month in the insurer example, against a £180k build). The value is predictability, compounding, and the relationship, not the unit price.

choices.text: Operating work needs the least skill, so rung 4 can be staffed cheaply with juniors and subcontractors.
choices.explanation: Monitoring, retraining, eval re-runs and cost tuning are real MLOps capability — and the run seat is where trust (and the next sale) lives. Staffing it as an afterthought burns exactly the relationship the rung exists to compound.

choices.text: Clients are not capable of operating the systems you build, so a run contract follows automatically from every build.
choices.explanation: Nothing follows automatically — the module says to *design* every rung-3 build to hand off into a run contract. Selling a build with no plan for who runs it is a named red flag: you've sold a system that will rot.

quiz.takeaway: Design every build to hand off into a run contract — recurring revenue is the durable-business signal, and project revenue alone is re-sold every quarter.

quiz.prompt: Which of these pricing decisions *protects* your margin rather than giving it away?

choices.text: Fixed price on a productised service — your delivery cost falls every repeat (templates, eval harness, checks built once) while the price holds, so margin rises with volume.
choices.explanation: This is the economics of reuse. On T&M you sell an hour once; on fixed-price productised work you keep every efficiency gain. It's why the reusable assets — templates, eval harnesses — are commercially precious: they turn delivery hours into product.

choices.text: T&M on a productised assessment, so the client only pays for the days actually used.
choices.explanation: Named explicitly as what NOT to do: T&M on a productised service gives away your reuse margin. You did the hard work of building the template once — billing by the day hands that efficiency gain to the client instead of keeping it.

choices.text: A fixed price quoted before discovery on a bespoke build, to win the deal with certainty.
choices.explanation: Pricing blind — another named anti-pattern. On a bespoke engagement the risk of a fixed price sits with you (you eat overruns), so committing before discovery means committing before you know the cost. Fixed or capped *after* discovery is the pattern.

choices.text: A flat monthly run fee on a managed service, so the client gets cost certainty regardless of adoption.
choices.explanation: Compute and token spend scale with adoption — a flat fee means you absorb the growth. The module's guidance: commit to *visibility and a cap*, not a fixed number. Cost certainty for the client at your expense is margin leaking by design.

quiz.takeaway: Choose the pricing model per service, not once for the offer — and protect reuse: fixed price on productised work is how delivery hours become product margin.

quiz.prompt: A client offers you 10% of measured fraud savings instead of a fixed fee for a fraud-detection build. Under what condition should you accept value-based pricing?

choices.text: Only if the outcome is isolated, instrumented and attributable to your work — a clean baseline and an agreed measurement method — otherwise sell the fixed-price system and let them keep the upside.
choices.explanation: Value-based pricing is the highest-margin model *and* the riskiest. You can only claim a share of an outcome you can attribute — which needs exactly the experimentation rigour of module 05. If the metric moved for ten other reasons, you'll fight over the counterfactual and lose.

choices.text: Always — it has the highest margin profile of the four models, so it should be your default.
choices.explanation: Highest *and riskiest*. Defaulting to it means routinely betting your margin on numbers you may not be able to defend. The module's rule: never bet your margin on a number you can't defend.

choices.text: Accept whenever the projected total exceeds the fixed fee you would have quoted.
choices.explanation: Projected value is paper value. The question isn't whether the number *could* be bigger — it's whether you can *prove* your share of it when the client's revenue has moved for many reasons at once. Without attribution, the bigger number never gets paid.

choices.text: Never — outcome-based pricing is a gimmick that serious consultancies avoid.
choices.explanation: It's a legitimate model with a legitimate niche: value that is large, measurable, and attributable to you. The error isn't using it — it's using it where the counterfactual is contested.

quiz.takeaway: Use value-based pricing only where the metric is isolated and instrumented; otherwise sell the fixed-price system that produces the value and let the client keep the upside.

quiz.prompt: Your pole's new pitch deck leads with the tech stack: frontier models, a vector database, managed pipelines, a cloud AI suite. What's wrong with this positioning?

choices.text: That entire layer is a commodity anyone can rent — differentiation must come from what the commodity can't supply: domain workflow templates, reusable eval harnesses, methodology, named accelerators, and proof.
choices.explanation: In 2026 the commodity layer is enormous and shared by everyone. Leading with it positions you as interchangeable. "Buy the commodity, build the differentiator" applies to your own offer: spend your build budget only where it creates defensible value you keep across clients.

choices.text: Nothing — buyers want to see proven technology, and a strong stack builds trust fastest.
choices.explanation: Buyers de-risk by buying *proof*, not technology lists: case studies with measured results, reference clients, named accelerators. "We've done this for three firms like you" builds trust fast; a stack slide says nothing your competitors' decks don't.

choices.text: The deck should list *more* technologies, to position the pole as a full-spectrum generalist.
choices.explanation: A generalist offer is easy to assemble and hard to sell — you compete on price against everyone. The niche offer wins: harder to assemble, far easier to sell, with reuse driving margin and domain proof commanding a premium.

choices.text: Only the vendor names are the problem — they'll be stale within a year and make the deck look dated.
choices.explanation: Perishable names are a cosmetic issue. The structural problem is leading with the rentable layer at all: the catalogue is your *capability*, the niche is your *go-to-market*. Lead with the niche, deliver from the catalogue.

quiz.takeaway: Your differentiation cannot be the technology — lead with the niche and the proof, and spend your build budget only on assets you keep across clients.

quiz.prompt: A scaling organisation's centralised AI CoE has become a bottleneck — business units complain it's an ivory tower detached from their problems, while use-case demand keeps growing. What do you recommend?

choices.text: Move to a hybrid hub-and-spoke: a central team owns the platform, standards, scarce skills and the run; embedded delivery squads in the business own the use case and the outcome — with that ownership split made explicit.
choices.explanation: This is where most successful poles land. The hub stops every squad re-buying the same vector store; the spokes stop the hub building things nobody asked for. The split that must be explicit: hub owns platform and run, spoke owns use case and outcome.

choices.text: Fully embed: dissolve the central team and place every data/AI person inside a business unit.
choices.explanation: Pure embedding has its own documented failure modes: re-invented wheels, inconsistent standards, duplicated infra, silos. You'd trade the ivory tower for ten teams each buying their own platform and shipping ungoverned systems.

choices.text: Keep the CoE centralised but add headcount until the bottleneck clears.
choices.explanation: The bottleneck is structural, not capacity. A bigger central queue is still a queue, and it does nothing for the detachment problem — the CoE still lacks the domain proximity that embedded squads provide.

choices.text: Declare embedded the target end state, since high maturity always means full federation.
choices.explanation: The module names *hybrid* as the common end state for scaling poles, not full federation. Embedded-only fits when domain depth dominates and you accept duplication — most organisations need the hub for standards, governance and shared platform.

quiz.takeaway: Hybrid hub-and-spoke is the common end state — the hub owns the platform and the run, the spokes own the use case and the outcome, and that split must be explicit.

quiz.prompt: Why does the module insist that an AI & Data pole's security posture must go beyond classic application security?

choices.text: Because the data, the models and the prompts are now part of the attack surface — poisoned datasets, backdoored weights, prompt injection — and the blast radius is asymmetric: one raw-PII call to an external API or one leaked key can be a reportable breach.
choices.explanation: That's the mental shift. A classic app is attacked through its inputs and infrastructure; an AI system is *additionally* attacked through training data, model supply chain, and prompts. Default: least privilege everywhere, and treat every external model and dataset as untrusted code.

choices.text: Because AI systems run on more infrastructure, so there are simply more servers to patch.
choices.explanation: Surface *area* isn't the point — surface *kind* is. The new vectors (data poisoning, model supply chain, injection) are categorically different from infrastructure patching, and classic controls don't see them at all.

choices.text: Because LLM vendors are inherently insecure, so anything touching their APIs is compromised by default.
choices.explanation: The discipline isn't vendor paranoia — it's process: read the vendor's data-retention terms before client data touches the API, and pseudonymise PII first. The named red flag is *your* pipeline sending raw PII out, not the vendor's existence.

choices.text: It doesn't need to — a firewall, pen tests and standard appsec cover an AI system like any other software.
choices.explanation: Standard appsec misses every AI-specific threat in the module's table: injection via retrieved content, training-data leakage, poisoned models from public hubs, over-broad RAG access. The pole needs an AI-systems inventory and controls those tools never check.

quiz.takeaway: Your data and your model are part of the attack surface, not just your code — least privilege everywhere, and treat every external model and dataset as untrusted.

quiz.prompt: Your pole hits 100% utilisation for the second consecutive quarter, and the sponsor congratulates you on the efficiency. What's the correct read?

choices.text: It's a red flag, not a trophy: zero slack means no pre-sales, no proof-building, no upskilling and no recovery — you're mortgaging next quarter's pipeline to flatter this quarter's number. Healthy poles target a band around 70-85%.
choices.explanation: Exactly the module's warning. Utilisation drives margin because people-cost is fixed — but the unbilled work (proposals, case studies, the dual-ladder upskilling) is what *creates future billing*. A pole pinned at 100% is eating its own pipeline.

choices.text: It's the goal — every unbilled hour is pure cost against a salary you owe regardless, so 100% is the margin-optimal state.
choices.explanation: The premise is true (idle hours are pure cost) but the conclusion is wrong. Maximal utilisation maximises *this quarter's* margin by starving the activities that generate the next one. High, not maximal.

choices.text: It's only a problem because of burnout risk — if the team is happy, 100% is fine to sustain.
choices.explanation: Resilience is one cost, but the documented mechanism is commercial: pre-sales, proof-building and upskilling are unbilled and essential. Even a tireless team at 100% produces no proposals, no case studies, and no pipeline.

choices.text: It means your rates are too low — raise prices until demand falls back to capacity.
choices.explanation: Pricing power comes from positioning and proof, not from a utilisation reading. The KPI is telling you about *slack design*, not price elasticity — and raising rates without protecting unbilled time still leaves no room for the work that compounds.

quiz.takeaway: Utilisation should be high but not maximal — the ~70-85% band funds billing *and* the unbilled work (pre-sales, proof, upskilling) that creates future billing.

exercises.title: From capability list to offer ladder
exercises.scenario:
You've just been named tech lead of a new six-person AI & Data pole inside a mid-size consultancy. The partners hand you two things: a list of everything the team "can do" (RAG assistants, churn models, data-quality work, GDPR gap reviews, demand forecasting, MLOps) and a glossy deck of **ten named services** — most of which the team has delivered zero times. Your first anchor prospect is a regional bank whose COO asked, vaguely, "can you help us with AI?"

The partners want a commercial design, not a tech plan.
givens: Team capabilities: RAG/knowledge systems, classical ML (churn, forecasting), data quality, GDPR/governance, MLOps — strongest track record in RAG and data-quality work
The bank: regulated, data siloed, no AI governance, an executive sponsor who is curious but unburned
Reference price points from your market: assessments €15-25k, discovery/PoC €25-40k, builds €120-200k, run retainers €6-10k/month
A productised assessment costs you ~€16k of effort to deliver the first time; the reusable template cuts that to ~€8k by the third delivery; you charge €20k fixed
tasks: Pick a **spine** of 3-4 services and place them on the four-rung offer ladder, with a pricing model per rung and a one-line justification for each pricing choice.
Design the bank's land-and-expand journey rung by rung with realistic prices, then compute the first-order **LTV** if the journey ends in an €8k/month run retainer expected to last 4 years.
Compute the **gross margin** on the productised assessment at first delivery and at third delivery, and state in one sentence what this says about productised vs bespoke.
Name two red flags in the partners' ten-service deck and what you'd do instead.
exercises.solution:
**Step 1 — the spine.** Pick what the team does best *and* what ladders: (1) **AI & Data Readiness Assessment** (rung 1, fixed price — productised, repeatable, near-constant cost, so fixed price keeps the reuse margin); (2) **Data Quality Audit / GDPR Gap Assessment** (rung 1, fixed price — same logic, and it's the natural entry for a regulated bank); (3) **Permissioned RAG build** (rung 3, fixed-or-capped *after* discovery — bespoke, so never quote fixed before you've scoped); (4) **Monitoring & retraining managed service** (rung 4, retainer — anything that must be operated is recurring by nature). Rung 2 (a RAG feasibility/eval engagement) bridges 1→3, also fixed price. Everything else on the partners' list becomes "yes, we can also do that" once trust exists — not a headline.

**Step 2 — the bank's journey and LTV.** Assess (3 wks, €20k fixed): readiness + data-quality scoring; output is the prioritised backlog — say it surfaces a strong "policy & procedure assistant" use case. Discover (4 wks, €30k fixed): RAG feasibility on their real documents, proving the answer-quality number on *their* data. Build (12-14 wks, €160k capped): permissioned RAG with access control and audit logging — the regulated-corpus version, because the bank's constraint is the differentiator. Run (€8k/month): monitoring, eval re-runs, retraining, cost tuning.

LTV on the retainer alone: €8k × 12 = **€96k/year**; over 4 expected years ≈ **€384k** — before any further builds. Total relationship: €210k of project work + ~€384k run ≈ **€594k unlocked by a €20k door-opener**. That is why the assessment is priced for conversion, not for its own margin.

**Step 3 — gross margin on the assessment.** First delivery: (20 − 16) / 20 = **20%**. Third delivery: (20 − 8) / 20 = **60%**. Same price, same deliverable — the template, checklist and report skeleton were built once and reused. One sentence: *productised fixed-price work converts delivery hours into an asset, so margin rises with volume, while bespoke work re-pays the same setup cost on every client and margin stays flat.*

**Step 4 — red flags in the deck.** (1) *Ten services, mostly never delivered* — a named red flag. Sell a service once bespoke, learn its real cost, **then** productise; a catalogue with one productised service you sell repeatedly beats a glossy ten-service deck. (2) *No designed hand-off into run* — if every service ends at delivery, there is no recurring revenue and every quarter starts at zero. Fix: every rung-3 service gets a run-contract clause in the proposal by default. (Also acceptable: generalist positioning with no niche; no pricing model per service.)

keyPoints: Spine of 3-4 services that *ladder into each other*, not a list of capabilities — with a pricing model per rung (fixed for productised, capped-after-discovery for bespoke, retainer for run) and justifications
LTV computed correctly (€8k × 12 × 4 ≈ €384k recurring) and used to argue the assessment is priced for conversion, not standalone margin
Gross margin computed at both points (20% → 60%) with the reuse mechanism named: cost falls while price holds
Red flags identified from the note: undelivered catalogue services, and builds with no run hand-off (or generalist positioning / single pricing model for everything)
pitfalls: Pricing the assessment for its own profitability — the door-opener exists to convert, and a thin deliverable kills trust and referrals
Quoting the build fixed-price *before* discovery — on bespoke work the overrun risk sits with you; cap it after scoping
Putting all ten services in the offer because the team 'can' do them — sell bespoke once, learn the cost, then productise
Designing rung 3 to end at delivery — no run contract means no recurring revenue and a system that rots
takeaways: The offer is a funnel: each rung produces the evidence, trust and backlog that sells the next, and rung 4 is where the consultancy becomes a business
Reuse is the margin engine — fixed-price productised services turn delivery hours into IP whose cost falls with every sale
rubric.transfer: This land-and-expand structure is the standard commercial pattern far beyond AI consulting: SaaS freemium tiers (free plan → paid seat → enterprise contract), law and audit firms (cheap compliance review → litigation/advisory retainer), and even internal platform teams pitching a CFO (small pilot → funded platform → permanent budget line). Wherever trust must be built before a large commitment, design a cheap, genuinely useful first transaction whose real return is the conversion rate into the relationship — and judge it on that, never on its own margin.

exercises.title: Pole health review: turn a KPI dashboard into an executive narrative
exercises.scenario:
Eighteen months in, your pole has grown to twelve people. The sponsor wants a board update next week. The outgoing lead drafted slides that list the nine models currently in production, with accuracy figures for each.

The PSA tool shows this quarter's KPIs:

| KPI | This quarter | Two quarters ago |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Utilisation | 97% | 82% |
| Gross margin | 28% | 29% |
| Recurring revenue share | 6% | 5% |
| Delivery success rate | 70% | 85% |
| Assessment → build conversion | 1 in 6 | 1 in 3 |
| NPS | falling | stable |

The partners' instinct is to celebrate utilisation and hire two more people.
givens: Twelve billable people; a typical consultant this quarter booked 155 billable hours of 160 available
Almost all revenue is project work; only one small run retainer exists
No demand-intake gate: requests arrive via partners' relationships and are accepted ad hoc
The board wants: is it working, where's the value, what's the risk, what's next and what does it cost
tasks: Diagnose the KPI pattern: explain the causal story that links utilisation, conversion, delivery success and margin — not six separate observations.
Verify the utilisation figure for the typical consultant and state the band you'd target instead, with the reason.
Propose three roadmap moves, each tied to the KPI it should repair.
Rewrite the board update as the four executive questions — and say what's wrong with the models-list draft.
exercises.solution:
**Step 1 — the causal diagnosis.** This is one story, not six numbers. Utilisation at 97% means zero slack — so nobody is doing pre-sales, proof-building or proper scoping. That shows up downstream twice: **assessment→build conversion halved** (no time to nurture the funnel, no case studies being produced) and **delivery success fell 85%→70%** (over-committed people, under-scoped missions — the module flags a falling success rate as over-selling or under-scoping). NPS follows delivery success down, which threatens referrals — the engine of land-and-expand. Meanwhile **margin is flat at ~28%** despite frantic billing because nearly everything is bespoke project work: no productisation (no slack to build templates) and almost no run book (recurring share stuck at 6%). The pole is sprinting on a treadmill: this quarter's billing is consuming the assets that produce next quarter's.

**Step 2 — utilisation arithmetic.** 155 / 160 ≈ **97%** — consistent with the dashboard. Target band: **~70-85%**. The reason is structural, not soft: people-cost is fixed, so billable hours drive margin, *but* the unbilled hours fund the pipeline (proposals), the proof (case studies from every assessment), the upskilling (the dual ladder), and recovery. 100% utilisation is a red flag that mortgages next quarter — exactly what the conversion and success numbers are already showing.

**Step 3 — three roadmap moves, tied to KPIs.**
1. **Stand up the run book**: add a managed-service clause to every live build and revisit recent deliveries to sell monitoring/retraining retainers. → Targets *recurring revenue share* (the durable-business signal) and stabilises *margin*.
2. **Productise the two services delivered most often** (build the assessment template and eval harness once). → Targets *gross margin* (cost falls while price holds) and frees capacity.
3. **Install the demand-intake gate** (one-page request, value × feasibility scoring, risk-tier and lawful-basis checks before work starts) and drop the utilisation target to ~80%. → Targets *delivery success* (no more ad-hoc over-commitment) and *conversion* (protected pre-sales and proof-building time). Note hiring two more people is *not* on the list: pouring capacity into an unmanaged intake just scales the problem.

**Step 4 — the board narrative.** Lead with trend, in their terms:
- **Is it working?** "Demand is strong and billing is full — but three health indicators (delivery success, conversion, NPS) turned down this quarter because we're running at 97% utilisation with no slack. We're correcting before it compounds."
- **Where's the value?** A small portfolio view: missions as bets with outcomes and proof — e.g. "the claims-triage build is live and measured at X; two assessments converted to builds worth €Y." Not nine models with accuracy figures.
- **What's the risk?** The honest register: over-utilisation eroding delivery quality, key-person risk at 97% load, near-zero recurring revenue making every quarter a cold start — plus what we declined and why.
- **What's next and what does it cost?** The three funded moves above, in € and quarters: productisation investment, run-book push with a recurring-revenue target, intake gate + utilisation band.

**What's wrong with the models list:** the board can't act on accuracy figures — a named red flag is "a pole reported to the board as a list of models instead of a portfolio with a P&L." Executives need trend, portfolio, risk and the next funded bet; models are how the work gets done, not what the business is.

keyPoints: A *causal* diagnosis linking 97% utilisation → no pre-sales/proof → falling conversion, and over-commitment → falling delivery success and NPS — not six disconnected observations
Utilisation verified (155/160 ≈ 97%) with the ~70-85% target band justified by what unbilled time funds
Roadmap moves each tied to a KPI: run book → recurring revenue, productisation → margin, intake gate + utilisation band → delivery success and conversion
Board narrative structured as the four executive questions, led by trend, with the models-list draft rejected for the documented reason
pitfalls: Celebrating 100%-ish utilisation as efficiency — it's the red flag driving the other declines
Answering the falling numbers with 'hire more people' — without an intake gate and slack design, added capacity scales the dysfunction
Reporting models and accuracy to a board instead of a portfolio-and-P&L narrative
Proposing fixes with no KPI linkage — the roadmap is a business roadmap expressed in the pole's KPIs, not a list of initiatives
takeaways: The pole is itself a product: run it on a small honest dashboard, and read the KPIs as one causal system, not a scorecard
Executive communication at pole level is portfolio and P&L — trend first, then value, then risk, then the next funded bet
rubric.transfer: Reading a KPI dashboard as a causal story rather than a scorecard is the universal operating-review skill: a factory running at 100% machine utilisation with rising defect rates, a hospital at 100% bed occupancy with growing readmissions, an engineering team at 100% sprint capacity with rising incident counts. In every case the same pathology — no slack → quality and pipeline erosion — and the same fix: target a utilisation band, protect the non-billable work that compounds, and report the *system* to leadership, not the inventory of outputs.
